---
title: "Benchmark datasets"
subtitle: "Load deterministic fixtures and score estimated betas against known truth."
jupyter: python3
execute:
  freeze: auto
---

## Available Fixtures

`fmrimod` ships small, deterministic datasets where the true betas are
known. They are ideal for learning and for sanity-checking a method: you
can fit a model and immediately ask how close the estimates are to the
truth, with no randomness between runs to muddy the comparison.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import fmrimod as fm

fm.list_benchmark_datasets()
```

## Inspect a Dataset

```{python}
name = "BM_Canonical_HighSNR"
summary = fm.get_benchmark_summary(name)

summary["dimensions"], summary["experimental_design"]
```

```{python}
data = fm.load_benchmark_dataset(name)
data.keys()
```

## Build a Design Matrix

```{python}
X = fm.create_design_matrix_from_benchmark(name, hrf="spmg1")
X.head()
```

## Fit OLS and Score It

The benchmark stores condition-level ground truth, so we drop the
intercept row before scoring. We solve the GLM with `np.linalg.lstsq`
here so the scoring is fully explicit and you can see every step; in a
real analysis you would use `fmri_dataset → fmri_lm`, which does the
same fit with the design provenance and contrasts attached.

```{python}
Y = data["Y_noisy"]
beta = np.linalg.lstsq(X.to_numpy(), Y, rcond=None)[0]
condition_beta = beta[1:, :]

performance = fm.evaluate_method_performance(name, condition_beta, "OLS")
pd.Series(performance["overall_metrics"]).round(4)
```

## Compare Estimated and True Betas

```{python}
true_beta = data["true_betas_condition"]
comparison = pd.DataFrame({
    "true": true_beta.ravel(),
    "estimated": condition_beta.ravel(),
})

comparison.corr().iloc[0, 1].round(5)
```

## Next

- [Simulation](simulation.qmd)
- [Linear model](linear-model.qmd)
